coaching.title: Today's Run
coaching.message: Easy 6–7 miles, Z2 (130–140 bpm), rolling terrain if available but no structured vert work. You're 91 days out in full taper (week 21 of 22), and your recovery score sits at 48/100—moderate but not critical. Yesterday's 5-miler at 133 bpm was solid; today is a shakeout to keep the legs loose and the aerobic system engaged without accumulating fatigue. Hold back; the long run Saturday is where you'll find your signal. If you're traveling for work today, a hotel treadmill at easy effort is acceptable—maintain consistency, not intensity.

coaching.title: Fuel Right
coaching.message: You logged 5.62 hours of sleep last night at 88.8% efficiency—solid, but the 30-day average of 5.03h tells me sleep debt is real. Prioritize carbs today: aim for 6–7g per kg of body weight to top off glycogen before the weekend's long run. Time your biggest carb intake (oats, rice, pasta) within 2 hours post-run; add 20–25g protein. Skip the junk; you need nutrient density. If you're in transit, pack overnight oats and a nut-butter packet—airports and meetings will rob you of fuel if you're reactive.

coaching.title: Recovery
coaching.message: Sleep is your limiting factor right now. Five-hour nights are below race prep minimum; aim for 7+ tonight. The Julian Alps 50K demands mental toughness at mile 30–40 when glycogen dips and fatigue stacks—sleep deprivation kills that reserve. Use the taper window to bank 30–60 minutes extra sleep for the next two weeks. If work travel is fragmenting your sleep, move non-critical meetings or decline late dinners. Your resting HR (62 bpm) and the recovery score (48) suggest you can handle today's easy run, but sleep is the non-negotiable lever right now.

coaching.title: Mental Edge
coaching.message: You're in the mental taper dip—energy is lower, doubt creeps in, and the distance (50K, 2,500m vert) feels heavier than it did at peak week. That's normal. Use these next 91 days to visualize the hardest sections: the long false summits, the sparse aid stations, the rocky descents in thin air. Walk the technical terrain with mental rehearsal during easy runs. By race day, your legs will have done the work; your mind needs the pre-recorded script. Talk to one teammate or training partner this week who has raced alpine ultras—borrowed confidence is real.
